Perspective

                                                                                                          Outlook on Agriculture
                                                                                                          1–8
Improved feeding and forages at a                                                                         ª The Author(s) 2020
                                                                                                          Article reuse guidelines:

crossroads: Farming systems                                                                               sagepub.com/journals-permissions
                                                                                                          DOI: 10.1177/0030727020906170
                                                                                                          journals.sagepub.com/home/oag
approaches for sustainable livestock
development in East Africa

Birthe K Paul1,2 , Jeroen CJ Groot2, Brigitte L Maass3 ,
An MO Notenbaert1,2, Mario Herrero4
and Pablo A Tittonell5,6,7


Abstract
Dairy development provides substantial potential economic opportunities for smallholder farmers in East Africa, but
productivity is constrained by the scarcity of quantity and quality feed. Ruminant livestock production is also associated
with negative environmental impacts, including greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, air pollution, high water consumption,
land-use change, and loss of biodiversity. Improved livestock feeding and forages have been highlighted as key entry point
to sustainable intensification, increasing food security, and decreasing environmental trade-offs including GHG emission
intensities. In this perspective article, we argue that farming systems approaches are essential to understand the multiple
roles and impacts of forages in smallholder livelihoods. First, we outline the unique position of forages in crop-livestock
systems and systemic obstacles to adoption that call for multidisciplinary thinking. Second, we discuss the importance of
matching forage technologies with agroecological and socioeconomic contexts and niches, and systems agronomy that is
required. Third, we demonstrate the usefulness of farming systems modeling to estimate multidimensional impacts of
forages and for reducing agro-environmental trade-offs. We conclude that improved forages in East Africa are at a
crossroads: if adopted by farmers at scale, they can be a cornerstone of pathways toward sustainable livestock systems in
East Africa.

Keywords
Sub-Saharan Africa, farming systems, improved forages, livestock feeding, bio-economic modeling, sustainable intensifica-
tion, technology adoption, systems agronomy, forage grass, dual-purpose forage legume


                                                                   1
                                                                     Tropical Forages Program, International Center for Tropical Agriculture

In Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), the primary aim is to                   In this perspective article, we argue that multidisciplin-
improve smallholder livelihoods, while mitigating negative        ary farming systems approaches are essential to under-
environmental impacts is a co-benefit through efficiency          stand the multiple roles and impacts of forages in
gains (Campbell et al., 2014). Improved livestock feeding         smallholder livelihoods. Systems approaches are needed
and forages have previously been highlighted as a triple-         that strive to overcome previous boundaries between dis-
win strategy toward achieving climate-smart agriculture,          ciplines (interdisciplinary) and between research and
increasing food security and resilience, and decreasing           practice (transdisciplinary) (Hieronymi, 2013). First, we
GHG emission intensities (Bryan et al., 2013; Peters              outline the unique position of forages in crop-livestock
et al., 2013; Thornton and Herrero, 2010). Climate change         systems and systemic obstacles to adoption that call for
mitigation by improved forages can be achieved through (i)        multidisciplinary thinking. Second, we discuss the impor-
increased carbon accumulation particularly in deeper soil         tance of matching forage technologies with agroecologi-
layers through conversion from cropland to perennial, sown        cal and socioeconomic contexts and niches, and systems
forages; (ii) reduced methane emissions from enteric fer-         agronomy that is required. Third, we demonstrate the
mentation through higher nutritional value and digestibility      usefulness of farming systems modeling to estimate multi-
of feed that reduces emissions per unit milk or meat pro-         dimensional impacts of forages and for reducing agro-
duced; (iii) lower nitrous oxide emissions through high           environmental trade-offs.

                                                                     Land availability and tenure is not conducive: In areas
Various studies from SSA have reported an inadequate              with high agroecological potential and population pressure,
supply of quality feed. In the East African subhumid              farmers need to weigh between various land uses and asso-
highlands, feed shortage is pronounced especially in              ciated opportunity costs. Food crops will often be priori-
the dry season(s) or during prolonged dry spells                  tized over forage crops to ensure food self-sufficiency of
(Lukuyu et al., 2009; Mutimura et al., 2015). In seven            the household. Cash crops, such as dry season horticultural
sites across West and East Africa, livestock milk yield           crops, are an attractive income-generating activity when
gaps ranged from 45% in Lushoto, Tanzania, 55% in                 markets are available (Makoni et al., 2013). In Kenya, milk
Nyando, and 40% in Wote, both in Kenya (Henderson                 and meat production could be increased by 51% and 71%,
et al., 2016).                                                    respectively, but 50–300% more cropland would have to be

important role in alleviating such constraints in quantity        of the most highly populated countries in SSA, allocating
and quality feed. Tropical forages include a wide variety         sufficient land to forage cultivation is a major challenge
of sown or planted grasses, annual and perennial herbac-          (Klapwijk et al., 2014a). Lack of land tenure has been
eous or dual-purpose legumes, and leguminous fodder               shown to discourage forage cultivation, especially for per-
shrubs and trees that are integrated into different agricul-      ennial forages (Njarui et al., 2017).
tural systems to increase livestock productivity. Due to             Entire production system needs to change: It requires a
their diverse properties, they can play various roles and         substantive production system change and cultural shift to

we are referring throughout this article to original names        harvesting, and capital for seeds and land in producing
as used in the publications cited. Grasses have been more         feed that was previously acquired “for free” are more
popular than legumes among farmers due to lower mainte-           reluctant to start growing forages. Such investment is
nance requirements for planting and weeding, less pest and        mostly common for food crops but not for feed (Thomas
disease pressure, their perennial nature, and soil protection     and Sumberg, 1995). Moreover, improved feeding needs
properties. Grasses are also regarded as more resilient and       to go hand in hand with a range of other technological
universally adapted than legumes (Peters and Lascano,             changes to achieve expected production response. A
2003). Napier grass (Pennisetum purpureum) is a C4 grass          farmer would have to improve the animal breed, provide
native to SSA and widely used in cut-and-carry systems in         drinking water, ensure veterinary services, and improve
East Africa due to its high herbage yields per unit area and      animal husbandry in order to reap benefits of higher milk
relative tolerance to intermittent drought. However, it           production from feed improvements (Ndah et al., 2017).
requires high soil fertility and is subject to disease pressure   The adoption of several technologies at the same time is a
including stunt and smut diseases (Negawo et al., 2017).          challenge to smallholder farmers lacking investment
There are other well-documented forage technologies:              capacity and access to knowledge.
leguminous fodder shrubs/trees including Calliandra                  Production intensification might not be primary
calothyrsus, Sesbania sesban, and Leucaena trichandra in          objective: Most fundamentally, an obstacle to adoption
East Africa (Place et al., 2009); and herbaceous legumes          might be that a farmer’s objective has not been well
(Stylosanthes guianensis, Stylosanthes hamata, and                defined (Sumberg, 2002). In SSA, farmers often manage
Mucuna pruriens) and dual-purpose legumes such as                 livestock according to the weighing of their functions.
cowpea (Vigna unguiculata) and groundnut (Arachis                 Production intensification may not be the main priority
hypogea) in West Africa (Kristjanson et al., 2005).               for farmers that primarily keep livestock for providing

hybrid cv. Mulato II, and Desmodium uncinatum cv. Sil-            or for cultural reasons (Thomas and Sumberg, 1995).
verleaf—silverleaf desmodium—or Desmodium intortum                Multifunctionality of livestock might provide incentives
cv. Greenleaf—greenleaf desmodium) has been a wel-                for keeping large livestock herds at low productivity
comed byproduct of the well-documented push–pull                  levels, instead of reducing stocking rates and investing
system (Kassie et al., 2018).                                     in increased productivity (Descheemaeker et al., 2016a).
   However, farmers’ adoption of tropical forage technol-
ogies remains below expectations (de Haan et al., 2006;
Owen et al., 2012). Often-cited reasons include the lack          Systems agronomy to match forages with
of awareness and knowledge, low support and investment

from national and local authorities, lack of available, acces-
sible and affordable forage seed and planting material, and       contexts

2017; Owen et al., 2012; Peters et al., 2003). Several            requirements and production objectives, underline
deeper-lying, systemic reasons can be identified that are         the necessity of matching forage technologies with

germination of Striga due to root exudates (Khan et al.,           which reduces comparability across sites. Forage agron-
2014). The smart use of agro-biodiversity in the push–pull         omy has been less resourced than that of other field
system is providing spatial niches for forage crops that have      crops, resulting in fewer publications and less estab-
shown to benefit farmers (Chepchirchir et al., 2018).              lished evaluation methods. Multi-locational, consistent,
    However, more systems agronomy is needed to produce            high quality, and inter-operational data are crucial for
robust socio-ecological niches for various systems that can        forage agronomy to keep pace with the challenge of
be scaled. Agronomy research, in general, has been criti-          scalability and the evolution of (big) data science, geos-
cized for lacking “scalability” by emphasizing local rele-         patial analytics, and decision support tools to produce
vance of research results. Researcher-controlled trials are        context-specific advice.
well suited to identify single yield-influencing factors and
to elucidate underlying mechanisms. However, they fail to
                                                                   Systems modeling for reducing
predict realistic performance under farmers’ conditions, as
yields are determined by the interplay of several factors          agro-environmental trade-offs
within environment, genotype, and management domains.              Improved forages are thus at a unique position of mixed
Only a small proportion of farmers will reach the average          farming systems, directly linking crop, livestock, and soil
yield under experimentation, owing to the large variability        components. Changes in livestock feeding can have mul-
of agroecological conditions and management that affect            tiple impacts on productivity, environmental, and liveli-
performance. Statistical methods continue evolving to con-         hood dimensions across various crop-livestock systems
sider and embrace this variability (Vanlauwe et al., 2016).        (Figure 3).
6                                                                                                 Outlook on Agriculture XX(X)


   Farming systems approaches and modeling can be used        Conclusions
to explore forage integration and relationships with vari-
                                                              In this perspective article, we have shown that improved
ous other components of farming systems and estimate
                                                              forages in East Africa are at a crossroads: if adopted by
their multidimensional impacts and trade-offs. Models are
                                                              farmers at scale, they can be a cornerstone of pathways
useful to study and predict the behavior and performance
                                                              toward intensified sustainable livestock systems in East
of agro-ecosystems. They can also reduce resource
                                                              Africa. Forages occupy a key role in smallholder farming
requirements from field and farm experimental research,
                                                              systems, linking soil, crop, and livestock components.
and they can help to formulate management recommen-
                                                              Changes in livestock feeding can have multidimensional
dations (Jones et al., 2017). Agricultural systems model-
                                                              impacts on farmers’ livelihoods in terms of productivity
ing has been applied to questions of system intensification
                                                              and environmental quality. Systemic characteristics,
and diversification beyond single crops and minimizing
                                                              including the need to change the entire production system
trade-offs and exploiting synergies between system com-
                                                              and multidimensionality of livestock, affect adoption of
ponents (Groot et al., 2017). Trade-offs influence the
                                                              improved forages and call for multidisciplinary thinking.
adoptability, impact, and sustainability of possible inno-
                                                              Further, forage technologies need to be matched with
vations and future pathways. Trade-off analysis often
                                                              agroecological and socioeconomic contexts to address
employs interdisciplinary, bio-economic models to
                                                              competition for land and fulfill various production objec-
address those multiple dimensions in one approach.
                                                              tives. Robust, “scalable” systems agronomy is needed to
Multi-objective optimization, in particular, is considered
                                                              develop context-specific advice and decision support on
a useful approach as farmers are not ultimate profit max-
                                                              socio-ecological niches for forages. Farming systems mod-
imizers but have to balance various functions of their
                                                              eling can be employed to estimate multidimensional
production system (Kanter et al., 2016; Klapwijk et al.,
                                                              impacts of forages and for reducing agro-environmental
2014b; Salmon et al., 2018). Quantitative systems model-
                                                              trade-offs. Translating modeling results into decision
ing can help to systematically explore trade-off frontiers,
                                                              advice, without losing sight of farming systems intrinsic
which can be expected to be different for farm types with
                                                              complexities, needs further development. Multidisciplinary
contrasting biophysical conditions and resource endow-
                                                              farming systems approaches are pivotal to bring tropical
ment (Groot et al., 2012). Changes in available technolo-
                                                              forages into wider use and to support sustainable livestock
gies, market conditions, and policies can lead to
                                                              development trajectories in East Africa.
adjustment of the efficiency frontiers and can, thus,
reduce the trade-offs between performance criteria such

as profitability and GHG mitigation (Descheemaeker
